%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Using Operational Amplifiers for System Realization

> **Section Objective:** Learn how op-amp circuits implement the multiplier, integrator, and adder blocks used in continuous-time system realization.

## Concepts In This Section

- Inverting op-amp transfer rule
- Scalar multiplier
- Op-amp integrator
- Weighted adder
- Polarity reversal in canonic realization
- Resistor and capacitor value selection

## 1. The Inverting Op-amp Transfer Rule

The ideal op-amp is analyzed using two key assumptions: the voltage at the negative input terminal is held at **virtual ground** (essentially 0 V), and essentially **zero current** flows into the op-amp input terminals due to very high input impedance.

Under these assumptions, all input current flows through the feedback path, giving:

- \(Z(s)\) — input impedance (element between source and summing node)
- \(Z_f(s)\) — feedback impedance (element between output and summing node)
- \(X(s)\) — input voltage, \(Y(s)\) — output voltage
- \(H(s) = Y(s)/X(s)\) — the transfer function

**When to use:** Any inverting op-amp circuit with one input impedance and one feedback impedance.

**Exam trigger:** "Find the transfer function of this op-amp circuit."

**Common misuse:** Forgetting the minus sign — the inverting configuration always produces a sign flip.

**Minimal example:** If \(Z_f = 2Z\), then \(H(s) = -2\).

$$H(s) = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{Z(s)}$$

## 2. Scalar Multiplier: Resistor Over Resistor

When both the input element and the feedback element are **pure resistors**, both impedances are constant (frequency-independent), so the circuit realizes a **constant-gain multiplier**.

- \(R\) — input resistor
- \(R_f\) — feedback resistor
- Gain is negative because the op-amp is inverting

**When to use:** Any block in the system diagram that requires multiplication by a constant.

**Exam trigger:** A desired gain such as \(-4\) or \(-10\), or a resistor-ratio design question.

**Common misuse:** Writing \(R/R_f\) instead of \(R_f/R\), or forgetting the negative sign and treating the gain as positive.

**Minimal example:** With \(R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega\) and \(R = 25\,\text{k}\Omega\):

$$H(s) = -\frac{100}{25} = -4$$

$$H(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R}$$

## 3. Op-amp Integrator: Capacitor in Feedback

Replacing the feedback resistor with a **capacitor** changes the feedback impedance to \(Z_f(s) = 1/(Cs)\), while the input remains a resistor \(Z(s) = R\). Applying the inverting rule:

$$H(s) = -\frac{1/(Cs)}{R} = -\frac{1}{RCs}$$

- \(R\) — input resistor
- \(C\) — feedback capacitor
- \(s\) — complex frequency variable
- The factor \(1/s\) is the **signature of integration**
- \(-1/(RC)\) is the integrator gain

**When to use:** Any realization block that needs an integrator \(1/s\), especially in direct-form continuous-time system diagrams.

**Exam trigger:** Capacitor in the feedback path.

**Common misuse:** Assuming any capacitor in the circuit creates an integrator. The textbook integrator specifically requires capacitor in feedback and resistor at input.

**Minimal example:** If \(RC = 1\), then \(H(s) = -1/s\).

$$H(s) = -\frac{1}{RCs}$$

## 4. Weighted Adder: Several Inputs, One Output

An **inverting summing amplifier** connects multiple input signals to the same op-amp summing node, each through its own resistor. Each input is scaled by its own gain before being summed.

- \(X_j(s)\) — the \(j\)-th input signal
- \(Y(s)\) — the combined output
- \(k_j\) — the gain applied to input \(j\)

The gain for each input branch is:

$$k_j = -\frac{R_f}{R_j}$$

where \(R_j\) is the resistor on input \(j\) and \(R_f\) is the shared feedback resistor.

**When to use:** A block diagram requires a weighted sum such as \(-4sW(s) - 10W(s) + X(s)\).

**Exam trigger:** Multiple input resistors feeding the same op-amp summing node.

**Common misuse:** Assuming all inputs share the same gain. Each input resistor independently sets its own weight.

**Minimal example:** With \(R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega\) and \(R_2 = 50\,\text{k}\Omega\), input 2 has gain \(k_2 = -100/50 = -2\).

$$Y(s) = \sum_{j=1}^{r} k_j X_j(s)$$

## 5. Polarity Reversal in Op-amp Realization

Every op-amp block in this section — multiplier, integrator, adder — is an **inverting** circuit. This means each stage flips the sign of the signal passing through it. When chaining multiple inverting stages to realize a system, the internal signal polarities may differ from the abstract canonic block diagram.

Consider the transfer function \(H(s) = (2s+5)/(s^2+4s+10)\). The canonic relation is:

$$s^2W(s) = X(s) - 4sW(s) - 10W(s)$$

After accounting for the polarity reversals introduced by the inverting op-amp blocks, this is rewritten as:

$$-s^2W(s) = -X(s) + 4sW(s) + 10W(s)$$

This rewritten form is what the physical op-amp adder actually implements.

- \(W(s)\) — output of the last integrator
- \(sW(s)\) — output of the first integrator (one stage upstream)
- \(s^2W(s)\) — the adder output (two stages upstream)

**Exam trigger:** Converting a block diagram to an op-amp circuit.

**Common misuse:** Copying the direct-form signs directly into the op-amp circuit without correcting for the inversions introduced by each stage.

$$-s^2W(s) = -X(s) + 4sW(s) + 10W(s)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="trap_exposure" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Inverting op-amp rule: \(H(s) = -Z_f(s)/Z(s)\) — feedback impedance over input impedance, always negative.
- Scalar multiplier: \(H(s) = -R_f/R\) — two resistors give constant gain; ratio is \(R_f/R\), not \(R/R_f\).
- Op-amp integrator: \(H(s) = -1/(RCs)\) — capacitor in feedback, resistor at input; \(RC=1\) gives \(-1/s\).
- Weighted adder: \(Y(s) = \sum_{j=1}^{r} k_j X_j(s)\) with \(k_j = -R_f/R_j\) — each input branch sets its own gain independently.
- Sign warning: op-amp blocks are inverting, so canonic diagram signs must be corrected before implementation — never copy signs directly.

*Next, feedback and control ideas use these realizations to analyze closed-loop system behavior.*
